answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation*, Volume II, offers a comparative ethnography of human "species" (races) and their varying degrees of physical, intellectual, and moral development, heavily tied to subsistence modes (hunting/fishing vs. agriculture), climate, and geography, rather than a strictly linear historical stadialism.**[^1][^2] He surveys contemporaneous peoples—e.g., copper-colored North Americans (hunters/fishers), Malays (mixed), Negroes (agricultural)—noting how hunter-fishers exhibit minimal property, elective chiefs, extreme individualism, vengeance-driven morals, perfidy, and frequent violence/famine, yielding chaotic "governments" based on strength.[^3][^4] Agriculturists show more property division, hereditary chiefs (limited authority), purer morals, and reduced scarcity/violence.[^5] This yields a quasi-stadial typology: least-developed (hunters) → more structured (agriculturists), with progress correlating to subsistence sophistication, moral refinement, and nascent authority.

**Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau Traité d'Économie Sociale*, Volume I, presents an explicitly progressive stadial history of liberty, defined as power over one's faculties via industry (skill) and morality.**[^6] He traces societal evolution through subsistence/modes of life: savage hunters (low liberty, violent, famine-prone); nomads/herders (slightly more, but rapine-heavy); sedentary slaves (masters live off labor); serfdom (peasants till soil for lords); privilege/monopoly (post-feudal castes); office-seeking (pre-industrial); purely industrial (universal labor/exchange, maximal liberty).[^7][^8] Liberty grows as violence wanes, property emerges, and industry/morals develop; hunter-savage life is least free (brutish, insecure), industrial most (peaceful, productive).[^9]

**Their typologies converge on subsistence as the driver of moral/governmental evolution and liberty-like traits (security, reduced violence).** Both depict hunter-savages as least advanced: minimal property/division of labor, elective chiefs (war/hunt-only), vengeance/perfidious morals, strength-based inequality, habitual scarcity/famine breeding egoism/cruelty.[^10][^11] Agriculture/sedentary marks progress: private property, hereditary (weak) authority, tempered morals, less vengeance/war (tribal peace via cultivation).[^12][^13] Divergences: Comte is synchronic/comparative (by race/climate), Dunoyer diachronic (universal progression to industry); Dunoyer emphasizes liberty explicitly, Comte implies it via development/security. Yet Dunoyer echoes Comte's ethnography (e.g., North American hunters), integrating it into his stages.[^14]

Comte's Vol II lacks Dunoyer's teleological industry endpoint or intermediate serfdom/privilege, focusing on pre-modern baselines; together, they align subsistence-stadialism with French liberal anthropology, prefiguring industrial liberty.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [Chapter 1](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/#p-ccd355).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [Chapter 14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/14-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-fe597e).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [Chapter 14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/14-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-de6422).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [Chapter 14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/14-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-2a0353).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [Chapter 14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/14-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-5e567c).

[^6]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).

[^7]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Introduction](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-e9a4bc).

[^8]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 11](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-f1f859).

[^9]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/05-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-pe/translation/#p-11bd3d).

[^10]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [Chapter 14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/14-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-69ea4f).

[^11]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/05-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-pe/translation/#p-24773b).

[^12]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [Chapter 14](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/14-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-ebfa80).

[^13]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 7](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07-ii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des/translation/#p-52489c).

[^14]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-d0df94).
